topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud, the subject line stark and unforgiving: "Event Cancellation." My fingers hovered over the screen, a cold knot tightening in my stomach. A surprise party. All the planning, the secret coordination, the giddy anticipation—it was all dissolving into this sterile notification. I reread the message three times, searching for a misplaced comma, a typo that might signify a mistake. Nothing. The silence in my small apartment became deafening, amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest. I slowly closed the laptop, the click of the lid sounding disproportionately loud in the quiet room. I stood by the window, watching the indifferent city lights, feeling a strange, heavy pressure building behind my eyes.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" The word escaped my lips before I could stop it. It was a thin, reedy sound, completely lacking its usual warmth. I stared at the printed invitation lying on the coffee table, the vibrant colors mocking the sudden emptiness of the room. My hands clenched into tight fists, the knuckles turning white against my skin. Every muscle in my neck felt rigid. I started pacing the length of the living room, a restless energy vibrating through me. I kept glancing at my phone, half-expecting a call that wouldn't come, a text that wouldn't offer an explanation. The air felt thick, suffocating, as if I couldn't draw a full breath without feeling the weight of everyone’s disappointment pressing down on my shoulders.

[story 3]

I picked up my phone, my thumb tracing the screen with unnecessary force. The notification was there, undeniable proof: the gathering was off. A slow, creeping wave of heat washed over my face, starting at my cheeks and spreading downward. I felt a distinct urge to curl inward, to make myself smaller, to disappear into the fabric of the sofa. I dropped the phone onto the cushion beside me with a soft, defeated thud. My jaw tightened, and I forced myself to take a deep, shaky breath, but it didn't settle. My gaze drifted to the framed photos on the wall, and for a moment, I couldn't manage the effort to keep my expression neutral. A slow, tight smile began to pull at the corners of my mouth, a gesture that felt utterly wrong.
